coredata.dc:title: Construction of green fluorescent carbon dots with high quantum yield for cancer cell recognition and Fe3+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Bing
Tao, Junting
Pan, Jiaqi
Li, Chaorong
Li, Fu
Zheng, Yingying
coredata.dc:description: Herein, green-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-step solvothermal method. After purified by precipitation and silica column chromatography procedures, the pure CDs had uniform particle size and excellent fluorescence performance. Upon excitation at 430 nm wavelength, the CDs exhibited the maximum emission at 530 nm with absolute quantum yield (QY) of 16.5% in water. The CDs showed low cytotoxicity, remarkable biocompatibility to three cell lines (MCF-7, QSG-7701, EC cells) and good photostability to PH. Besides, different labeling distributions of CDs in cancer cells (MCF-7) and normal cells (QSG-7701) were achieved so that they could be used to identify cancer cell. Moreover, the CDs had good selectivity to detect Fe3+ ion in aqueous solution. The above hold them have great potential application in bioimaging and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Purification
Selective fluorescence imaging
Fe3+ ion detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and HRTEM (inset) image, (b) size distribution of CDs.
XPS spectra of synthesized CDs (a) full XPS spectrum, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s (d) O 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption, Insert: photographs of CDs under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) The influence of PH on the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots of CDs. (d) Zeta potential of CDs in different solution.

(a) Fluorescence emission change of CDs in the presence of different metal ions. (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+ (0–20 μM). (c) Linear relationship between the fluorescence of CDs and the concentration of Fe3+ ions (0.1–4 μM). (d)The fluorescence lifetime curves of CDs in the absence and presence of 20 μM Fe3+.
Viability of MCF-7, QSG-7701 and EC cell lines with different concentrations of CDs.
Fluorescence images of the MCF-7 cell and QSG-7701 cell after incubated with CDs under the concentration of 100 μg/ml.
Fluorescence images of the MCF-7 cell and QSG-7701 cell after incubated with CDs under the concentration of 200 μg/ml.
Schematic illustration of preparation of CDs for the application in sensors (towards Fe3+ ion) and bioimaging (towards MCF-7, QSG-7701 cells).

head.ce:title: Construction of green fluorescent carbon dots with high quantum yield for cancer cell recognition and Fe3+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, And Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzho, 310018, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: And Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: a Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzho, 310018, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Metallurgical Products Quality Inspection Station of Guang, Xi Zhuang Autonomous Region, Nanning, 530013, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Metallurgical Products Quality Inspection Station of Guang

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Metallurgical Products Quality Inspection Station of Guang, Xi Zhuang Autonomous Region, Nanning, 530013, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, green-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-step solvothermal method. After purified by precipitation and silica column chromatography procedures, the pure CDs had uniform particle size and excellent fluorescence performance. Upon excitation at 430 nm wavelength, the CDs exhibited the maximum emission at 530 nm with absolute quantum yield (QY) of 16.5% in water. The CDs showed low cytotoxicity, remarkable biocompatibility to three cell lines (MCF-7, QSG-7701, EC cells) and good photostability to PH. Besides, different labeling distributions of CDs in cancer cells (MCF-7) and normal cells (QSG-7701) were achieved so that they could be used to identify cancer cell. Moreover, the CDs had good selectivity to detect Fe3+ ion in aqueous solution. The above hold them have great potential application in bioimaging and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new kind of fluorescent nanoparticles with a diameter less than 10 nm, have received tremendous attention in the past ten years [1,2]. CDs have been proved the most promising alternative to traditional fluorescent materials, like organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots. Owning to their excellent water solubility, remarkable biocompatibility, environmentally friendliness, low cytotoxicity, abundant functional groups [3,4], CDs have many applications in bioimaging [5–8], photocatalytic [9,10], biosensors and chemical sensors [11–13], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [14–16], and drug delivery [17].

According to previous reports, a series of CDs which could emit photoluminescence (PL) ranging from blue to red have been obtained by hydrothermal or solvothermal, and microwave assistant methods [18–22]. In these methods, heteroatoms (N, S, P and B) doping were commonly and extremely effective means to regulate physicochemical, fluorescence and biological features of CDs [23,24]. Tian found different dimensions of conjugated sp2-domain leaded to different emission colors from blue to red [25]. Miao found changing the degree of graphitization could also get different emission colors CDs [26]. However, due to residual by-products and solvent molecular, they were difficult to be applied in bioimaging.

CDs have relatively strong fluorescent properties, endowing it fast, sensitive and effective in metal ions detection and cancer diagnosis [27–31]. Iron ion (Fe3+), as an essential element in human body, is basic structure of hemoglobin, myoglobin and many cofactors involved in enzyme activities and plays crucial role in cytochrome and oxygen transportation exchange at the cellular level [32–36]. Excessive concentration of Fe3+ has adverse effect to human health and immunity and results in severe mental illness, hemochromatosis, neurodegenerative diseases, etc. Therefore, it is necessary to develop an effective and sensitive method for Fe3+ detecting. Cancer remains a major threat to human health [37]. Distinguishing between normal cells and cancer cells is important for early cancer diagnosis. Usually, CDs need to use tumor markers or biomolecules to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells. For instance, Zhang et al. [38] prepared folic acid (FA) conjugated green luminescent carbon dots to identify folate receptor (FR) overexpressed cancer cells. Gao et al. [39] introduced CDs/Fe3+ system to identify cancer cell according to it's sensitive to glutathione (GSH). Both of tumor markers or biomolecules will reduce the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots and affect the quality of fluorescence imaging.

In this work, we chose wildly used citric acid as carbon source and urea as nitrogen source to prepare green-emitting nitrogen-doped CDs by solvothermal method (Scheme 1 ). After purified by precipitation and column chromatography, pure CDs were successful obtained with a high absolute QY of 16.5% in water and brilliant biocompatibility. Purification also endowed CDs with excellent fluorescence properties and good water solubility. The green fluorescent CDs had different imaging effect in two cells. More CDs concentrated in nucleus in MCF-7 cell and less CDs evenly distributed in QSG-7701 cell. Without the aid of the tumor markers, the cancer cell was easily distinguished from two cell lines by fluorescence imaging. Furthermore, the CDs had a good sensitive to Fe3+ with a wide concentration range of 0.1–4 μM and a detection limit of 73 nM.

Citric acid (CA) and urea were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). DMF was obtained from Hangzhou Gaojing Chemical (China). AgNO3, CuCl2, CaCl2, Zn(CH3COO)2, MnCl2·4H2O, SnCl2·2H2O, Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O, Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O, FeCl3, KCl, NaCl, Pb(NO3)2 and LiClO4 were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and 3-(4, 5-Dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Phosphate buffered solution (PBS) and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) were obtained from Hyclone (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and penicillin-streptomycin solution were purchased from Gibco Co. Ltd., (USA). All solutions were prepared with deionized water with 18.25 MΩ cm (Millipore).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded in the region 500 to 4000 cm−1 on a Nicolet FT-IR 5700 spectrometer. The morphology of CDs was performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM JEM-2100). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained by Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer. The zeta potential was obtained using Nano-ZS 90. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured using a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. The composition of CDs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements performed on Kratos Axis Ultra system. Cells images were obtained by confocal laser scanning microscope FV100-IX81 and cell viability was measured by microplate reader ELx800. The Edinburgh FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrometer was used to assay the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs. The absolute fluorescence QY was measured with a calibrated integrating sphere on the C-9920-02 quantum efficiency measurement system.

CDs were synthesized through solvothermal process which was similar to previously described procedure but followed by two-step purification means [26]. In general, 0.58 g CA and 0.6 g urea were added into 10 ml DMF and stirred to be clear solution. Then the mixture was transferred into 25 ml polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves and heated at 160 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the precipitate was obtained by centrifuging the black-red solution at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Then a mixture of petroleum ether and ethyl acetate in a ratio of 3:1 was poured into the supernatant. The resulting precipitate was further purified by column chromatography. The eluent was methanol and dichloromethane. The solvent was removed by rotary evaporation to obtain a brown powder of CDs.

Detection of Fe3+ was carried out in PBS buffer solution (0.1 M, PH = 6.0). Different concentration of Fe3+ from 0.1 μM to 20 μM was added to 100 μl (200 μg/ml) of CDs solution respectively. Then the mixtures were diluted to 1 ml. After the mixtures were incubated for 3 min at room temperature, the fluorescence data was collected when the excitation wavelength was fixed at 430 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence date was also examined to evaluate the selectivity after the CDs (100 μg/ml) were incubated with Fe3+ and other metallic ions (Ca2+, K+, Na+, Li+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pd2+, Sn2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Ni2+ and Fe3+) with the concentration of 50 μM.

Cancer cell line (MCF-7) and two normal cell lines (EC and QSG-7701) were cultured in DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin at 37 °C in humidified 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. Afterwards, the cytotoxicity of the CDs was usually measured by MTT assay. For MCF-7, QSG-7701 and EC cells, firstly, 1 × 104 cells per well were seeded in 96-well plate (100μl per well) and cultured overnight until they were totally adhered to plate. Then, a series of different concentration (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250 μg/ml, respectively) of CDs solutions were added into each well and incubated for another 24 h.Four parallel samples were created in each group. Cells without the adding CDs were cultured as control. Thereafter, 20 μl of MTT (5 mg/ml) regent was added into each well, and the cells were further incubated for 4 h. Finally, the supernatant was removed and 150 μl DMSO was added into each well. The absorbance of each mixture was measured by microplate reader ELx800 at 490 nm.

The cellular imaging of CDs was carried out using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). Firstly, MCF-7 and QSG-7701 cells were seeded in 20 mm glass bottom dishes and cultured for 24 h at a density of 2 × 105 per dish in 2 ml of DMEM medium. Then, the medium was replaced with 2 ml fresh DMEM medium containing different concentration of CDs (100μg/ml, 200 μg/ml, respectively). After incubation at 37 °C for 4 h, the supernatant was removed and the cells were washed with PBS three times. Then, 100μl of 4% fixative solution was added into each dishes to fix cell morphology. Waiting for 10 min, the cells were further washed with PBS three times. Finally, 1 ml PBS was added into the cells on glass slides for confocal fluorescence imaging with the excitation of 488 nm.

CA and urea, as small molecular raw materials with simple structure, were easily dehydrated and carbonized into CDs by solvothermal treatment. Nitrogen-doping not only improved the quantum yield, but also endowed the CDs hydrophilic functional groups. After purified and lyophilized, residual by-products and solvent molecular were removed and brown powder of CDs was obtained. Besides, there was no precipitation or aggregation appearing to CDs aqueous solution at room temperature in three months, indicating good water solubility and stability of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the morphology and size distribution of CDs was legible displayed in TEM image. From the HRTEM image, the lattice fringes spacing of CDs was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphene. As shown in Fig. 1b, the spherical CDs were monodispersed with diameter ranged from 2 to 4.5 nm and the average size was 3.12 nm.

To investigate functional groups of CDs, FT-IR was employed. As shown in Fig. 2 , the peaks centered at 2934, 1709, 1674, and 1614 cm−1, corresponded to the stretching vibration of CH2, CO, CN, CC, respectively, suggesting the aromatic structure of CDs. In addition, two broad absorption peaks located at 3423 and 3203 cm−1 were ascribed to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively. XPS analysis was further confirmed the composition and structure of CDs. As indicated in Fig. 3 a, the full survey of XPS of CDs showed three peaks at 285,400 and 532 eV, which corresponded to the elements of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. And the atom percentage of elements was 78.07%, 7.26% and 14.66%, respectively. Fig. 3b illustrated that the high resolution C 1s spectrum had four peaks located at 284.5, 286.4, 287.1 and 288.7ev, respectively, which could be attributed to CC/C–C, C–N, C–O, CO bonding, respectively. Fig. 3c illustrated that the N 1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 399.1, 400.1 and 401.1eV, representing pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively. Fig. 3d displayed the spectrum of O 1s contained two peaks at 531.9 eV and 532.4 eV, which belonged to C–O and CO, respectively. The above date manifested that there were many hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as hydroxyl group, carboxyl group and amino-group, endowing CDs excellent water solubility. The zeta potential of the CDs was measured to be −9.8 mV in deionized water, indicating there were more carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 d, the potential of CDs was further investigated in PBS buffer at different PH. At PH 6, the zeta potential was −3.23 mV, whereas this value increased to 0.24 mV owing to the protonation of carboxyl groups at pH 5. This result was consistent with above analysis that there were many hydrophilic carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CDs.

The fluorescence properties of CDs were almost the most important for their application in biological imaging. Therefore, the optical properties of CDs were additionally measured by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra at room temperature. As shown in the insert of Fig. 4a, strong green fluorescence from CDs aqueous solution (right) could be seen under UV lamp (365 nm), while the CDs aqueous solution (left) showed slight yellow color under daylight illumination. As shown in Fig. 4a, the absorption peak of CDs at 415 nm was usually ascribed to n-π* transition of CO [40]. Fig. 4b revealed that the emission wavelength of CDs (centered at 530 nm) was hardly changed with the different excitation wavelength (from 410 to 480 nm). The optimal excitation wavelength was centered at 430 nm which was close to the absorption wavelength. These results confirmed excitation-independent behavior of as-prepared CDs, consistent with the uniformity of CDs size. Fig. 4c showed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs had almost no change when PH changed from 3 to 11, which also confirmed the stability of CDs. It had been measured the absolute quantum yield of CDs in aqueous solution was 16.5%, which was higher than previously reported [41]. The high fluorescence quantum yield appeared not only because of the doping of nitrogen, but also the further purification process.

Different metal ions were selected to display the change of fluorescence intensity of CDs, including Ca2+, K+, Na+, Li+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Ni2+ and Fe3+, shown in Fig. 5 a (F0 indicated the fluorescence intensity of CDs and F indicated the fluorescence intensity of CDs in presence of metal ions). The blank group was served as a comparison. It was clear that CDs had the higher selectivity for Fe3+ ions. To confirm the sensitive of CDs to Fe3+ ions, 200 μg/ml CDs solution was mixed with the different concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 20 μM. As shown in Fig. 5b, the emission spectra excitated at 430 nm wavelength revealed the fluorescence quenching curve of the concentration of Fe3+ ion to CDs. A standard linear between ΔF= (F0–F) and Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0.1 to 4 μM had a correlation coefficient of 0.99 in Fig. 5c. The limit concentration of detection (LOD) was 73 nM, calculated from 3δ/S (δ was the standard deviation of the blank sample and S was the slop of the standard linear). In order to determine the quenching mechanism between CDs and Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ were measured. As shown in Fig. 5d, the average lifetime of CDs was 5.96 ns while the average lifetime CDs/Fe3+ system was 3.79 ns. This difference could obviously confirm that Fe3+ induced dynamic quenching of CDs. According to the results of previous studies [42–44], the phenomenon was ascribed to the combination of Fe3+ ions and functional groups (-OH, –COOH, –NH2) on the surface of CDs, which led to the formation of CDs/Fe3+ complex. From the FT-IR spectra of green CDs and CDs with Fe3+ ion (after detection) shown in Fig. S1 of Supporting Information, it could be seen that oxygen-containing functional groups on CDs disappeared and nitrogen-containing functional groups on CDs were weakened. Thus, the electron transfer which took place in CDs/Fe3+ system led to the fluorescence quenching of CDs.

To explore the feasibility of CDs application in bioimaging, cytotoxicity is an indispensable factor. As shown in Fig. 6 , the MTT assays of the cell viability studies suggested that the CDs revealed negligible cytotoxicity towards MCF-7, QSG-7701 and EC cells. Even at high concentration of CDs up to 250 μg/ml, the viability of cells was maintained above 80%. Therefore, the as-synthesized CDs possessed the excellent biocompatibility and were adequate for biological applications.

The application of CDs in cell imaging has been widely reported, but few of them explored the effect of pure CDs in different cells. Based on the previous analysis, we found that CDs were very suitable for bioimaging. To ensure this, MCF-7 and QSG-7701 cells were selected for comparison experiment. Firstly, the aqueous solution of CDs was distributed to two cell lines with the concentration of 100 μg/ml. After incubation for 4 h, the live cells were imaged with a confocal microscope under a single 488 nm laser excitation. As shown in Fig. 7 , MCF-7 cell exhibited bright green fluorescence. It could be seen that as-prepared CDs penetrated into cells and distributed in cytoplasm and nucleus. Moreover, the fluorescence in the nucleus was stronger than that in the cytoplasm which was similar with previous reports [45,46]. However, it was clearly that fluorescence in QSG-7701 cell covered the entire cell. This significant difference between these two cells fluorescence imaging allowed us to easily identify cancer cells and normal cells.

To our knowledge, the PH value in cancer cell is lower than normal cell, especially in lysosomes. For MCF-7 cell, when CDs accumulated in lysosomes, the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs would be protonated. As shown in Fig. 4d, the change of value of zeta potential in PBS buffer with different PH had proved it. Due to the action of protonation, the PH in lysosomes increased, which led to water penetration into lysosomes and caused swelling and disintegration, thus allowing CDs to flow from lysosomes into the nucleus. For QSG-7701 cell, the cell morphology was clearly visible, and the fluorescence was evenly distributed throughout the cell. Therefore, we were able to distinguish cancer cell from these two types of cells by fluorescence imaging analysis. A comparative experiment to further verify the endocytosis of different cells was shown in Fig. 8 . When we increased the concentration of CDs to 200 μg/mL and observed the cells in a larger field of view with the same incubation time, it was clearly that in vitro cellular imaging was different between these two types of cells, which was consistent with the previous results. Though, the distinguish between cancer cell and normal cell was really existence, we don't consider that the combination of our synthesized CDs with a certain different substance in cancer cells or normal cells resulted in difference in vitro imaging of two types of cells. Of course, not all unmodified CDs had similar consequence. After purification, the surface of the CDs had a small size and abundant functional groups, which was important for cell uptake. At the same time, based on the fact that the endocytosis of cancer cells was stronger than normal cells, the effect of fluorescence imaging in cancer cells was obviously stronger than that in normal cells, which was also important for CDs to distinguish cancer cell from these two types of cells [47].

In summary, we had successfully developed green-fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs by solvothermal route with the absolute quantum yield (QY) up to 16.5% in water. After two steps of purification, the obtained CDs possessed excellent water solubility and biocompatibility. In addition, it exhibited excitation-independent emission in fluorescence spectrogram and good PH stability. These CDs were employed as a potential fluorescent probe for in vitro cellular imaging of MCF-7 and QSG-7701 cells. We could see that the CDs were mainly concentrated in the nucleus of MCF-7 cells and less CDs distributed throughout the entire cell of QSG-7701. According to this fluorescence microscopy imaging experiments results, we could distinguish cancer cell from these two types of cells. The as-prepared CDs also enabled high sensitivity towards Fe3+ ion with the detection limit of 73 nM as the Fe3+ could combine with amino, carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs. The excellent selectivity of unmodified CDs in cancer cell recognition and Fe3+ detection, which will further expand its application in medical diagnosis and environmental detection.

We certify that there is no conflict exiting in the submission of this manuscript, and it is not under consideration for publication elsewhere. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described is original research that has not been published previously. All the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51802282) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ17F040004).
